title: Anomaly Detection in Action: Fraud, Fault, and Failure
concept: Anomaly detection is a critical line of defense in the real world — protecting bank accounts from credit card fraud, detecting industrial machinery failures before they happen, and stopping network intrusion attempts.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a major advantage of unsupervised anomaly detection over supervised?

options.text: It can run immediately on new systems without needing a history of labeled failures
It is always 100% accurate and never flags normal points by mistake
It does not require any mathematical calculations to run

exercises.explain: Exactly! Unsupervised anomaly detection learns normal patterns directly, so you don't need to wait for a history of labeled failures to protect your system.

cards.title: A transaction in the middle of the night
cards.body: Your credit card is suddenly charged $900 for electronics in a city 2,000 miles away, at 3:00 AM. Within seconds, you get a text: 'Did you make this purchase?' That isn't luck — it's an anomaly detection model running in real-time on the payment network.

cards.title: Three main real-world arenas
cards.body: Anomaly detection is the default approach for three critical applications: financial fraud (spotting suspicious spend), predictive maintenance (finding machine failures before they happen), and network security (spotting hackers or unusual traffic).

cards.title: The mechanic who knows a machine's healthy purr
cards.body: Imagine a factory mechanic who's worked with a pump for years. They know exactly how it sounds, smells, and vibrates when healthy. The moment they hear a tiny high-pitched whistle, they shut it down — that whistle is an anomaly. By detecting it early, they save the factory from a catastrophic breakdown.

cards.title: Network intrusion: Spotting the silent thief
cards.body: Hackers rarely break in with loud alarms; they try to blend in. But anomaly detection models monitor network traffic (data transferred per second, login times). If a user account suddenly downloads 50 gigabytes of data at midnight — when normal downloads are 5 megabytes — the model flags it immediately.

cards.title: Every anomaly isn't a threat
cards.body: It's easy to assume every flagged anomaly is a hacker, a broken part, or fraud. In reality, anomalies are simply unusual. A sudden surge in website traffic could be a hacker (intrusion) or just a popular news story (normal viral hit). Models flag outliers, but humans or secondary systems often make the final judgment.

cards.title: Real-World Anomaly Use Cases
cards.body: What anomaly detection prevents in different industries:

left.text:
Detects credit card fraud
Identifies account takeovers
Blocks fake reviews

right.text:
Finds factory machine wear
Blocks network intruders
Flags server sensor faults

exercises.prompt: An energy company installs sensors on wind turbines to monitor vibration. They want to flag unusual vibration patterns that indicate a part is about to crack. What use case is this?

options.text: Predictive maintenance (finding wear and tear before failure)

exercises.explain: Correct! Monitoring sensors to predict equipment failure before it happens is predictive maintenance.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Anomaly detection models can only flag credit card fraud; they cannot be used for server or network security.
exercises.explain: False! Anomaly detection is widely used for network security, server health monitoring, and detecting hackers.

exercises.sentence: Predictive ___ uses anomaly detection to spot machine faults before they turn into complete breakdowns.
exercises.explain: Predictive maintenance saves money by finding small machine faults before they lead to complete failure.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario represents an anomaly detection system flagging a potential security intrusion?

options.text: A corporate database server suddenly starts exporting massive files to an external address at 2:00 AM, which it has never done before
A user resets their password by answering three security questions correctly
A database lists its table rows in alphabetical order

exercises.explain: Correct! A massive data export in the middle of the night is a strong deviation from normal server behavior, indicating a potential intrusion.

exercises.prompt: A bank's anomaly detection model blocks your credit card when you buy a coffee in a foreign airport during a vacation. The purchase was legitimate. Why did the model flag it, and how should we categorize this outcome?

options.text: The transaction was a true anomaly (unusual location and context compared to your normal spending), resulting in a false positive (flagging normal behavior as suspicious)
The transaction was not an anomaly, resulting in a true positive
The model has failed completely and should be deleted immediately
The transaction is a false negative because the card was blocked

exercises.explain: Exactly! The purchase was highly unusual (an anomaly in your location history), leading the model to flag it. Since it was actually legitimate, this is a false positive — a common tradeoff in high-security anomaly detection systems.
